how-i-self-hosted-gmail-mcp: Giving an AI agent write access to your inbox is exactly the setup that deleted 200+ of a Meta researcher's emails in early 2026 — so the real story here is doing it safely. The official claude.ai Gmail connector can read and draft but can't archive, label, or trash, so I built a self-hosted Gmail MCP server (one Python file plus a small OAuth script, on the MCP Python SDK and Google's Gmail API) with the modify scope — deliberately capped at trash (recoverable for ~30 days), never permanent delete. It took about two hours to build, then six weeks of a recurring auth failure whose root cause was Google's Testing-vs-Production OAuth mode: an unpublished test app expires its refresh token every 7 days, and publishing it fixed it for good.
how-i-run-parallel-claude-code-sessions: Ran several Claude Code agents at once in the same repo without them clobbering each other, using git worktrees (a built-in git feature that gives one repo multiple working copies, each on its own branch) plus cmux, a small open-source shell tool that manages the whole worktree lifecycle with one-word commands: new, start, ls, cd, merge, rm. One agent per branch means zero file conflicts, and it runs on both a Mac and an always-on home Linux server on a single Max subscription. The honest caveat: the parallelism comes from your own terminal tabs, not auto-split panes — and running four agents at once only pays off when the tasks are genuinely independent; otherwise the context-switching costs more than it saves.
what-claude-code-actually-costs-me: Built a weekly self-audit of every automated job that calls Claude on a home server — a hand-maintained JSON registry of each job (model, billing bucket, rough calls and tokens per week), a Python drift scanner that flags any new or changed AI job, a cost estimator, and a Sunday-morning email and phone digest. The surprise: real pay-as-you-go spend is under $2 a month (a handful of small Haiku jobs on a metered API key), while the bulk of usage rides a flat-rate subscription that would notionally cost around $14 a month but isn't billed per call. The first drift scan also caught two Claude-calling jobs the manual inventory had missed — the whole reason to audit instead of guess.
how-i-self-healed-schwab-oauth: Built a market-data pipeline that survives Schwab's 7-day OAuth token expiry without human babysitting. Schwab's API tokens hard-expire every week by design; instead of a manual reminder, a daily cron freshness check notices when the token is a couple of days from dying and renews it automatically, degrading gracefully and emailing (via Resend) only when a real person is genuinely needed. The renewal is built around one clean attempt — a hard lesson from an earlier project where letting automation retry a confused login got an account soft-locked — so it submits once and stops the instant it hits anything unexpected. The brokerage account behind it holds under a dollar, existing only to unlock the free data feed.
how-i-built-self-hosted-push-alerts: Replaced slow email alerts with instant phone push notifications by self-hosting ntfy (an open-source app that turns a plain web request into a push) on a home Linux server, so nothing leaves the network. The server is reachable only over Tailscale — a personal VPN where only your own logged-in devices can join — with UFW firewall rules making the service invisible to the public internet, deny-all access control, and a token kept in a permissions-locked file. Publishing an alert is a single web request any script can make. The main gotcha was a boot-order crash: the service tried to bind to the VPN address before Tailscale was up, fixed by correcting the startup dependency. First live use was an AC-power-loss alert that fires the moment the server drops to battery.
how-i-built-api-server: Built a personal API server on a headless Linux box using Express, systemd, and three layers of security (UFW LAN-only firewall, API key auth, port knocking) — six endpoints that let you trigger jobs, check server health, and pull reports with a single HTTP request instead of opening an SSH session. The fourth post in the Alienware series: after setting the server up, locking it down, and making it observable, this one makes it controllable. The entire codebase is one JavaScript file and one 12-line systemd service config.
how-i-built-cron-ops: Grew a home Linux server's crontab from 3 lines (Garmin recap, uptime check, heartbeat) to 100+ lines of scheduled automation spanning market briefings, game bot modules, security audits, batch task queues, and weekly reports — all orchestrated by cron with wrapper scripts, comment-header organization, and the dead-man's-switch pattern for catching silent failures. The key lessons: cron's minimal environment breaks scripts that work in a terminal (fix with a global PATH header), dependent Sunday jobs need per-input fallbacks, and shared OAuth tokens across concurrent scripts need file-locked token managers.
how-i-automated-trophymanager: Built a Python bot in 10 days (~15 hours across 10 sessions) that automates a TrophyManager.com soccer club — 9 cron jobs handling market scanning, bid sniping, lineups, training, sales, and a Claude-powered self-grading module. The biggest lesson wasn't technical but operational: for the first two weeks, most 'automated' actions never actually fired because Claude confidently mapped endpoints and response shapes that didn't match reality — the code ran without errors but the game state never changed, proving that AI overconfidence with undocumented APIs requires verifying outcomes in the real system, not just checking that scripts execute.
how-i-built-market-daily: Built a self-sending daily market briefing in ~8 hours across 6 sessions over 20 days — a Python script on a headless Linux server scrapes market data, asks Claude Haiku to synthesize a one-sentence 'why markets moved' thesis, formats an HTML email with news bullets, economic calendar, and options chains, then delivers it to subscribers via Resend at 8am every weekday. The subscriber system is just a Supabase table with token-based unsubscribe and two Next.js API routes — no user accounts, no passwords, no login page.
how-i-built-fruit-exchange: Built a community fruit tree map (Fruit Exchange) in ~6 hours across 3 sessions — Leaflet + OpenStreetMap for the interactive map, Supabase with 4 flat tables for listings/requests/exchanges/verification tokens, Resend for owner email verification, and a Harvest Moon SNES pixel-art aesthetic using Press Start 2P font. The design constraint of pixel fonts being unreadable below ~9px forced cleaner, more concise UI copy, and the biggest technical headache was Leaflet's 'Map container is already initialized' error caused by React strict mode double-firing effects.
how-i-built-server-alerts: Built a full observability stack for a headless home Linux server using cron, bash, Resend, and Claude — website uptime checks every 5 minutes, nightly Fail2ban reports, Garmin failure detection, Supabase health checks, a Claude-generated weekly changelog, and a healthchecks.io dead-man's switch for when the server itself goes offline. The key insight: each individual alert is simple (20–45 minutes to build); the value comes from having all of them running together so that silence becomes the signal for 'everything is fine.'
how-i-secured-linux-server: Hardened a home Linux server using five independent security layers — UFW default-deny firewall, Fail2ban brute-force banning (3 strikes, 24-hour ban), SSH key-only authentication with passwords disabled, port knocking via knockd to hide SSH from public scanners, and an ~/.ssh/rc login alert that emails instantly on any non-LAN connection. The layered approach means breaking one control doesn't compromise the others, and the full stack took about two hours to implement — with the most important rule being: always test changes from a second open SSH session before closing the one you're in.
how-i-setup-headless-linux: Repurposed a 2011 Alienware M11x R3 gaming laptop into an always-on headless Linux server in ~3 hours using Linux Mint, SSH, and one systemd config change (HandleLidSwitch=ignore) — total hardware cost was $0 since the laptop was already owned. The critical setup order: install Linux, allow SSH through UFW, set a DHCP reservation for a static LAN IP, copy SSH keys, then add a ~/.ssh/config alias so 'ssh alienware' is all it takes to connect from anywhere on the home network.
how-i-built-search: Built a self-updating site search with dropdown previews by extracting post metadata into a shared source file and writing a build script that auto-generates the search index—eliminating the brittleness of hand-maintained JSON. The key insight: ten minutes of upfront refactoring (centralizing the posts list) paid for itself by enabling automation that scales as the site grows, avoiding silent failures when new content gets added but forgotten in search.
how-i-automated-garmin-recaps: This writer built an automated system that pulls daily Garmin health data via Python API, generates AI-written recaps using Claude CLI, and emails them every morning at 7am using macOS launchd scheduling—taking ~2.5 hours across two sessions to prototype, debug, and deploy. The core insight: health data collection tools exist, but the friction of checking dashboards kills consistency; packaging the data as actionable morning emails removes that friction entirely.
how-i-replaced-google-forms: Built a custom contact form in 2 hours using Claude Code, Supabase, and Resend—replacing a generic Google Form with a branded component that stores submissions in a database and sends instant email notifications. The key win: using Claude Code's terminal-based file editing was 4x faster than browser-based workflows, cutting setup time from 8–10 hours (first Supabase project) to 2 hours by eliminating copy-paste friction and leveraging prior knowledge of the database layer.
how-i-built-greetings: Built a dynamic greeting system that rotates 50+ time-aware and day-aware messages on the homepage, replacing 8 static words with contextual variations (e.g., "coffee brewing" at 7am on Tuesday vs. "late-night building" at midnight). The key learning: ruthlessly trim first drafts (Claude generated 25–35 word greetings that needed cutting to 8–12), avoid packing all context into every variant, and iterate on copy more than code—6 rounds of refinement took 90 minutes but made the difference between over-engineered and actually human.
how-i-built-footer: Built a production-ready footer component for joseandgoose.com in 25 minutes using Claude Code (terminal-based AI with direct file access), 7x faster than the same workflow with Claude.ai in-browser. Key learning: eliminating manual file-switching friction between Claude and your editor dramatically accelerates design iteration, especially for self-contained components with no backend complexity.
how-i-built-numerator: A non-developer built Numerator, a live multiplayer game based on a game theory puzzle, in ~8–10 hours across 4 sessions using Claude as a pair programmer, Supabase for the database, and Vercel for hosting. The key lesson: locking down the game spec with Claude's clarifying questions upfront caught a critical bug (30% vs 50%) and saved hours of rework, proving that AI pair programming works best when you invest time in defining requirements before writing code.
gemini-grades: The author had Google's Gemini AI independently grade the difficulty of building joseandgoose.com to validate whether "beginner-friendly" was accurate—it confirmed the assessment, but revealed that while design and deployment are genuinely easy, environment setup and DNS configuration require patience and troubleshooting rather than coding knowledge. The real hurdle isn't technical skill but comfort with terminals, multiple platforms, and iterative fixes, plus a paid Claude subscription ($20/mo).
how-i-built-this: A non-developer built a fully deployed custom website in ~6–7 hours across three sessions using Claude to generate code, Next.js + Vercel for hosting, and free tools for analytics and DNS. The key insight: AI can now write complete, production-ready websites with mobile responsiveness and deployment config, compressing what normally costs $5k–$20k and requires hiring into an afternoon learning project. The main friction points were environment setup and debugging Terminal commands, but once the local dev server ran, iterating on design and features became straightforward prompt-and-paste cycles.
how-i-built-tldr: Built AI-generated TL;DR summaries for every post by generating them once at publish time via Claude Haiku, storing results in static JSON, and bundling with the build—eliminating runtime API calls, latency, and scaling costs. The architectural decision (build-time generation vs. real-time API calls) proved more valuable than the feature itself, and a silent environment variable failure early on showed the importance of defensive error handling in unattended scripts.
how-i-upgraded-search-to-vectors: The author upgraded their site's search from keyword-matching to semantic vector embeddings using pgvector, Supabase, and Hugging Face, enabling queries like "firewall" to return relevant posts even without exact word matches. The implementation layers semantic search as a fallback when keyword search returns zero results, preserving speed for obvious queries while adding meaning-based understanding where needed. Content vectors are generated locally at build time and queried against a Postgres database with HNSW indexing—a production-ready semantic search system built in ~2 hours with mostly free tools.
how-i-built-content-pipeline: Built an AI-powered content pipeline that generates topic tags via Claude Haiku, surfaces related posts using Supabase vector embeddings, and computes accurate reading times—all at build time with zero runtime overhead. Reused existing architecture patterns (TL;DR script + vector embeddings) to add discoverability across 18 blog posts in ~1 hour, replacing manual tags and estimated reading times with automated, taxonomy-validated classifications.
how-i-built-ask-goose: Built Ask Goose, a RAG chatbot grounded in 291 embedded content chunks that answers questions about my work and site—it streams Claude Haiku responses via a floating widget, powered by pgvector similarity search with keyword boosting and page-aware context injection. The system converged six earlier content infrastructure projects (search indexing, semantic embeddings, summaries, structured data pipelines) into a single conversational API that cost roughly $0.01 per query and took ~6 hours to ship, validating that vector storage was the linchpin enabling real-time retrieval-augmented generation.
grading-the-grader: We built a local AI grader to score chatbot conversations on accuracy, helpfulness, tone, and fallback behavior, then discovered the 1.5B parameter model was essentially broken—giving 82% perfect scores and misreading its own rubric. Upgrading to a 7B model revealed the real performance: the smaller model wasn't discriminating at all, while the larger one properly distributed scores and caught nuanced failures the first grader missed entirely.
